abstract: The structure and configuration of reaction centers, which fundamentally govern catalytic behavior, often undergo dynamic transformations under operating conditions; however, strategies to exploit these features for enhancing catalytic function remain elusive. Here, we demonstrate a facile light-activation strategy using a TiO2-supported Cu catalyst to regulate the dynamic restructuring of Cu active sites during low-temperature methanol steam reforming. Under dark conditions, the catalyst undergoes thermal deactivation as metallic Cu transforms into inoperative Cu2O. Upon illumination, photoexcited charge carriers from the TiO2 support drive a structural restoration of the Cu sites back to their active metallic state, resulting in a substantial boost to both activity and stability. Under low-intensity solar irradiation, the optimized Cu/TiO2 catalyst achieves a H2 production rate of 1724.1 μmol g⁻¹ min⁻¹, outperforming most conventional photocatalytic and thermocatalytic processes. By leveraging strong light-matter-reactant interactions, we achieve in situ manipulation of the Cu active sites, demonstrating the feasibility of real-time functionalization of catalysts to maintain peak performance.
